governing_bodies.name: Austin City Council

At least 8 candidates declared: Steven Brown, Alexandria Anderson, Naguib 'Simon' Nahas (pro-housing), Misael Daguan Ramos, Kyra Lorena Rogers, Amber Karessa Goodwin (assistant DA), plus 2 others who have appointed treasurers. Filing period: July 20-Aug 17.

governing_bodies.meeting_schedule: Thursdays, 10am, Austin City Hall, 301 W 2nd St
governing_bodies.public_comment_process: Sign up online at austintexas.gov/speakup by noon the day before, or register in person. 3 minutes per speaker. Remote participation available.

governing_bodies.public_comment_process: Online registration opens 8pm two nights before meeting, closes 8am day-of. Remote participation by phone. In-person also available.

upcoming_elections.race: Travis County Commissioner Precinct 4 — Democratic Runoff
candidates: George Morales III (former Constable Pct 4, 20 yrs Travis County)
Susanna Ledesma-Woody (AMD PM, 14-yr Del Valle ISD trustee)
upcoming_elections.relevance: Directly represents your precinct. March 3 primary: Morales 40.17%, Ledesma-Woody 35.18%. No Republican running — winner takes seat. Very low expected turnout = outsized individual vote impact. HIGH PRIORITY.
upcoming_elections.action: Verify voter registration. Research both candidates' positions on development, transit, and county budget. Consider volunteering — small canvassing effort could shift outcome in a low-turnout runoff.

candidates: Steven Brown (ex-Save Austin Now co-chair, anti-developer framing, $6K raised, residency concerns)
Alexandria Anderson (MLK Neighborhood Assn president, retired pro athlete)
Naguib 'Simon' Nahas (pro-housing, supports smaller lot sizes — most explicitly YIMBY candidate)
Misael Daguan Ramos (ran for D1 in 2022, has name recognition)
Kyra Lorena Rogers (apartment renter, was not yet registered voter when filed)
Amber Karessa Goodwin (assistant DA, Travis County DA's Office)
upcoming_elections.relevance: Your city council district. Harper-Madison is term-limited. Open seat for first time in 8 years — at least 8 candidates now in the field. 20 candidates total across all 5 open council seats (D1, D3, D5, D8, D9). Simon Nahas is the most explicitly pro-housing candidate. Brown's Save Austin Now ties remain a YIMBY red flag. Goodwin brings prosecutorial experience. Filing period: July 20-Aug 17.
upcoming_elections.action: Evaluate all declared candidates' housing positions. Simon Nahas advocates smaller lot sizes and more building — most aligned with YIMBY priorities. Research Goodwin's policy positions. Watch for additional entrants after filing opens July 20. Consider early engagement/donation to most-aligned candidate.

key_political_dynamics:
Austin politics center on a housing/growth vs. neighborhood preservation tension. The current council leans pro-development with Mayor Watson providing a pragmatic anchor, but faces organized NIMBY opposition on specific projects.

Key April 2026 developments: (1) ⚡ FY2027 BUDGET CRISIS: Forecast released April 16 projects $16.8M in social services cuts — permanent supportive housing, workforce development, adult education all targeted. $26.4M projected deficit under no-new-revenue tax rate (voters rejected increase Nov 2025). Current-year cuts already at $5.28M ($2.7M public health, $1.46M HSO). This directly threatens HSO strategic plan funding and affordable housing programs. (2) TENANT PROTECTIONS: Council expanding eviction prevention at affordable rental housing (April 17 committee briefing). City redirecting rent assistance from direct payments to legal advocacy and negotiated settlements — portal will not reopen. (3) AHSO is dramatically expanding encampment sweeps starting this summer — 42 city staff across 6 full-time HEM teams. CRITICAL: outreach workers will NO LONGER be embedded in sweep teams. Advocates call it criminalizing homelessness. 700+ 311 calls/month driving expansion. Unhoused residents report losing all possessions in sweeps. HSO Director Gray expected to present plan to council in early May work session. (4) APD met its target response time (10 min 44 sec) for Priority 0/1 calls for the first time since August 2023. 157th cadet class begins APRIL 21 (2 days away) with 65 cadets. Integral Care hiring 23 more mental health specialists. (5) Bond Election Advisory Task Force (BEATF) final recommendation expected late April/May — $687-700M bond package. Last window to influence allocation. (6) Density Bonus Code (DBC) council vote expected MAY 28 — 5 tiers, 0-60 feet extra height in exchange for affordable housing. Commissions reviewing now. (7) WAYMO: Still failing to stop for school buses despite months of work with Austin ISD (Planetizen April report). NTSB investigating. Also went wrong-way in San Antonio-area school zone April 2. Separately, launched pothole data-sharing partnership with Waze (April 13). AV/emergency services joint committee hearing April 29. (8) Project Connect: TX Supreme Court STILL hasn't ruled — 68 days since Feb 10 oral arguments. $60M design-build contract awarded. Mayor Watson blocked ATP's $47M downtown office deal. (9) $50M+ approved for affordable housing initiatives on April 9. West Campus development approved April 12. (10) Council approved 'Strong Local Commerce Initiative' April 2 — front yard businesses pilot.

Prior March developments: Pew confirmed Austin's 16% rent decline (steepest in US). HOME Initiative working. HSO strategic plan adopted March 26 (650 shelter beds, $101M ask). Missing middle zoning advanced. Convention center petition rejected. Pct 4 runoff set: Morales vs Ledesma-Woody, May 26.

BUDGET-HOUSING NEXUS: The $16.8M social services cut forecast creates a direct tension with the $101M HSO funding ask and the bond package. If the bond doesn't include significant affordable housing and social services allocations, the budget cuts could undermine the strategic plan before it's fully implemented. The BEATF recommendation is now even more consequential.

The Nov 2026 city council elections (Districts 1, 3, 5, 8, 9) will shape Austin's direction on housing for the next 4 years. District 1 is the most consequential for 78702 residents — 8+ candidates in an open seat for the first time in 8 years. Travis County is solidly blue; meaningful political competition is within the Democratic primary. The May 26 Pct 4 runoff is the next actionable election. Project Connect's TX Supreme Court ruling remains the single biggest infrastructure question for Austin's future.

immediate.action: Verify voter registration for May 26 runoff
immediate.llm_prompt: I need to verify my voter registration status in Texas for the May 26, 2026 Travis County Commissioner Precinct 4 Democratic runoff. I live in zip code 78702 (Austin, District 1). Walk me through the steps to check my registration at votetexas.gov, and tell me the key deadlines I need to be aware of for this runoff election. Also explain what a county commissioner does and why this low-turnout runoff matters for housing and transit policy.

immediate.detail: Compare positions on development, transportation, county budget priorities
immediate.llm_prompt: I need to research the candidates in the May 26, 2026 Travis County Commissioner Precinct 4 Democratic runoff in Austin, TX. Help me build a comparison framework. I care most about: (1) housing and development policy — do they support density and new construction? (2) transportation infrastructure — do they support Project Connect and transit investment? (3) county budget priorities — are they fiscally responsible while investing in infrastructure? Create a table template I can fill in as I research each candidate, with specific questions I should look for answers to.

this_month.action: Engage on 2026 Bond — BEATF final recommendation imminent
this_month.detail: The Bond Election Advisory Task Force's $687-700M recommendation expected late April/May. CONTEXT: FY2027 budget forecast projects $16.8M in social services cuts and $26.4M deficit — making the bond allocation even more critical for affordable housing and transit. Submit comments at speakupaustin.org.
this_month.reason: Budget cuts make bond allocation the primary funding mechanism for housing and social services. $700M shapes every policy domain.
this_month.llm_prompt: The Austin Bond Election Advisory Task Force (BEATF) is presenting its $687-700M bond recommendation to the Mayor and Council. Meanwhile, the FY2027 budget forecast (released April 16) projects $16.8M in social services cuts — permanent supportive housing, workforce development, and adult education are all targeted. The city faces a $26.4M projected deficit. Six working groups cover: affordable housing, facilities/assets, community engagement, parkland/open space, stormwater, and transportation/electrification. I'm a pro-housing, pro-transit YIMBY in 78702. Help me draft a concise public comment for speakupaustin.org that: (1) advocates for maximizing the affordable housing and transportation allocations, (2) references the budget cuts as making bond investment in housing even more urgent, (3) cites the HOME Initiative's success (16% rent decline) and Project Connect's construction timeline. Keep it under 300 words.

this_month.action: Track FY2027 budget impact on housing and social services
this_month.detail: FY2027 forecast released April 16: $16.8M social services cuts, $5.28M already cut in current year ($2.7M public health, $1.46M HSO). Permanent supportive housing and workforce development targeted. $26.4M projected deficit. Council budget hearings upcoming.
this_month.reason: Budget cuts directly threaten HSO strategic plan ($101M funding ask) and affordable housing programs
this_month.llm_prompt: The City of Austin's FY2027 budget forecast (released April 16, 2026) projects $16.8M in social services contract cuts targeting permanent supportive housing, workforce development, and adult education. The current year already cut $5.28M ($2.7M public health, $1.46M from Homeless Strategy Office). The city faces a $26.4M projected deficit under a no-new-revenue tax rate (voters rejected an increase in Nov 2025). Meanwhile, the HSO strategic plan asks for $101M in additional funding and the city is considering a $687-700M bond. I'm a 78702 resident who cares about housing, homelessness services, and fiscal sustainability. Help me understand: (1) How do these budget cuts interact with the HSO strategic plan commitments? (2) What are the key budget hearings where public comment matters? (3) What alternative revenue sources exist if the tax rate is constrained? (4) How does the bond package relate to the operating budget cuts?

this_month.action: Monitor District 1 council race — 8+ candidates now
this_month.detail: The field has grown to at least 8 candidates. Simon Nahas is the most explicitly pro-housing (supports smaller lot sizes, more building). Amber Goodwin (assistant DA) is new. Evaluate all candidates on housing positions.
this_month.llm_prompt: I'm tracking the Austin City Council District 1 race for November 2026. The field now has at least 8 candidates: Steven Brown (anti-developer, Save Austin Now ties), Alexandria Anderson (MLK Neighborhood Assn), Simon Nahas (pro-housing, wants smaller lot sizes), Misael Ramos (ran in 2022), Kyra Rogers, Amber Goodwin (assistant DA), plus 2+ others. I'm a pro-housing YIMBY voter in 78702. Help me create a comparison framework to evaluate each candidate on: (1) housing/density position, (2) transit support, (3) fiscal policy, (4) public safety approach. Filing deadline Aug 17, election Nov 3.

this_month.action: Write public comment on golf course rezoning
this_month.detail: Track when public hearings are scheduled. This is the biggest single housing opportunity in Austin. Be ready to submit public comment when hearings open.
this_month.llm_prompt: I need to write a public comment for the Austin City Council supporting the rezoning of the Jimmy Clay and Roy Kizer golf courses for residential and mixed-use development. I'm a resident of 78702 (District 1). My key points are: housing supply is Austin's most pressing need, city-owned land is the lowest-friction path to new housing, and transit-oriented development along future transit corridors should be prioritized. The site is 445 acres and could yield 5,000-15,000 housing units depending on density. Please draft a 2-3 minute spoken comment (about 400 words) that is specific, evidence-based, and avoids generic talking points.

this_month.action: Track DBC vote — Council expected May 28
this_month.detail: The new citywide Density Bonus Code (DBC) has 5 tiers adding 0-60 feet of extra height in multifamily/commercial zones in exchange for affordable housing. Several commissions reviewing now. This is the 'supertall-friendly density bonus overhaul' that builds on HOME Initiative.
this_month.reason: Key zoning reform vote in 6 weeks

medium_term.detail: Highest-alignment org for your priorities. One visit to evaluate fit.
medium_term.llm_prompt: I'm planning to attend my first AURA (Austinites for Urban Rail Action) monthly meeting in Austin, TX. I'm a pro-housing YIMBY interested in getting involved in local housing and transit advocacy. Help me prepare: (1) What should I expect at a typical AURA meeting? (2) What questions should I ask to evaluate whether this org is a good fit for me? (3) How can I introduce myself and my interests effectively? (4) What are AURA's current priority campaigns I should be familiar with before attending?

medium_term.action: Sign up for Austin Monitor newsletter
medium_term.detail: Best local political coverage, keeps you informed between pipeline runs.
medium_term.llm_prompt: I want to stay informed about Austin, TX local government and politics. I'm signing up for the Austin Monitor newsletter. What other Austin-specific news sources, newsletters, and social media accounts should I follow to stay informed about: city council actions, planning commission decisions, Project Connect updates, housing development news, and Travis County politics? Give me a prioritized list of the top 10 sources with links where possible.

strategic.detail: The runoff will have ~8-12% turnout. A small organized group (5-10 people) doing targeted outreach in your precinct could meaningfully shift the outcome. 10 hours over 4 weekends.
strategic.llm_prompt: I want to organize a small group (5-10 people) to do voter outreach for the May 26, 2026 Travis County Commissioner Precinct 4 Democratic runoff in Austin, TX. Expected turnout is only 8-12%, so a small organized effort can meaningfully shift the outcome. Help me create: (1) a 4-weekend canvassing plan covering the weeks before May 26, (2) a recruitment message I can send to friends and neighbors, (3) a script for door-knocking that focuses on getting people to show up for a low-profile runoff, (4) logistics checklist (walking lists, materials, water, etc.). I have about 10 hours total to invest across 4 weekends.

strategic.action: Early engagement in District 1 open seat race
strategic.detail: The Nov 2026 District 1 open seat is a once-in-8-years opportunity. Early engagement with a pro-housing candidate (or recruiting one) has maximum leverage now before the field solidifies.
strategic.llm_prompt: Austin City Council District 1 has an open seat for the first time in 8 years in the November 2026 election. I'm a pro-housing YIMBY resident of 78702 who wants to help ensure a pro-building candidate wins. Help me think through: (1) What does the ideal District 1 council member look like on housing, transit, and development policy? (2) How do I identify and evaluate potential candidates early? (3) What's the most effective way to engage with candidates before the field solidifies — should I be attending forums, donating early, volunteering, or something else? (4) If no good candidate emerges, how would I go about recruiting one? What's the timeline and what resources are needed?
